turns.content: I need you to help me make sense of something before I lose my composure entirely. The Ministry of Justice has just published the latest legal aid expenditure figures. Have you seen them?
I haven't seen the specific figures you're referring to, but I'm happy to help you work through whatever's caught your attention. What are the numbers showing?

Criminal legal aid spending has dropped in real terms again. We're now at roughly 40% below 2010 levels when you adjust for inflation. Forty percent. And the government has the audacity to talk about access to justice as though it's something they value.

That's a staggering decline. A 40% real-terms reduction over fifteen years would inevitably affect the quality and availability of legal representation. Are you seeing the impact directly in your own practice, or is this more about the systemic picture?

Both. I do privately funded defence work primarily, but I still take legal aid cases — I always have, on principle. The rates are an insult. I had a solicitor ring me last week about a case, serious fraud matter, multi-handed. The legal aid rates on offer meant I'd effectively be working at less than minimum wage once you factor in preparation time. And this is specialist advocacy we're talking about, not shelf-stacking. No disrespect to anyone who stacks shelves, but the skill set is rather different.

That's a real tension. When the rates don't reflect the complexity and skill required, it creates a two-tier system where legally aided defendants get less experienced or less resourced representation. Are you finding that fewer barristers at your level are willing to take on legal aid work?

Precisely. The juniors can't afford to do it because they're drowning in debt from training. The seniors won't do it because the rates are beneath them — and I understand that, even if I find it disheartening. So who's left? The people in the middle who still believe in something, grinding themselves down. I've watched colleagues leave the criminal Bar entirely. Good advocates. People who cared. Gone to commercial work or left law altogether.

It sounds like a slow hollowing out of expertise in criminal defence. The irony is that criminal law is where the stakes are highest for individuals — liberty, reputation, everything. What do you think a realistic fix would look like? Is it purely about funding levels, or are there structural changes that could help?

Funding is the foundation. You can restructure all you like but if the money isn't there, it's rearranging furniture in a burning building. The CLAIR recommendations were a start but they didn't go far enough and implementation has been glacial. What I'd want to see is legal aid rates indexed to inflation at a minimum, with proper recognition of preparation time. The current system pretends that a barrister can prepare a complex case in a fraction of the time it actually takes. It's dishonest accounting.

Indexing to inflation would at least stop the erosion. The preparation time point is crucial — if the funding model systematically underestimates prep time, it's essentially building in a subsidy from barristers' unpaid labour. Have any of the professional bodies been effective in pushing for these changes? The Bar Council, the Criminal Bar Association?

The CBA has been vocal. They organised the action in 2022 which did produce some movement. But there's a limit to what industrial action achieves when the public doesn't fully understand the issue. People hear 'barristers on strike' and think we're all earning six figures complaining about champagne budgets. They don't see the junior tenant in a bedsit in Tottenham preparing a murder case for the equivalent of twelve pounds an hour. The narrative is broken and nobody in government has any incentive to fix it.

The public perception gap is a real obstacle. There's a persistent image of barristers as uniformly wealthy that makes it hard to build sympathy for reform. It might take more sustained public storytelling — real examples of what legally aided defence actually looks like day to day — to shift that narrative. Is there anything specific you're trying to do with these figures, or did you mainly need to articulate the frustration?

Bit of both, honestly. I've been asked to speak on a panel next month about the future of criminal defence and I want to have the numbers sharp. But yes, sometimes one simply needs to say it out loud to someone who'll listen without glazing over. Thank you for that.

Any time. For the panel, if you want, I can help you pull together a concise briefing — key statistics, timeline of cuts, comparison with other common law jurisdictions, that sort of thing. Having the data tightly organised tends to land better than passion alone, though the passion clearly isn't something you're short of. Just let me know the format and I'll put something together.

A comparison with other jurisdictions would be quite useful, actually. I know Australia and Canada have had their own issues with legal aid funding, but I'd like to understand how England and Wales compares. Do we spend less per capita?

England and Wales historically had one of the more generous legal aid systems among common law jurisdictions, which is part of why the scale of the cuts is so striking. In broad terms:

- **England and Wales** has seen legal aid expenditure fall from around £2.5 billion in 2010 to significantly less in real terms. The Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 (LASPO) removed entire categories of civil legal aid and squeezed criminal rates.

- **Australia** has a mixed federal-state model. Legal Aid Commissions receive both Commonwealth and state funding, but reports from bodies like the Productivity Commission have consistently found that funding falls well short of demand. They have similar problems with private practitioners declining legal aid work due to inadequate rates.

- **Canada** follows a federal-provincial split as well. Some provinces fund legal aid relatively well; others have severe shortfalls. Ontario, for instance, has faced repeated crises in legal aid funding.

- **The United States** is a useful comparator for sheer scale of underfunding. Public defender offices in many states carry caseloads that would be considered unsafe by any professional standard. The Sixth Amendment right to counsel is a constitutional guarantee that is, in practice, frequently honoured in the breach.

The per capita comparison is difficult to pin down precisely because of differences in how costs are categorised, but the trajectory in England and Wales is among the steepest downward curves in the common law world. That's a powerful point for your panel — we had a system that was the envy of many jurisdictions, and we've actively dismantled it.

That is useful. The contrast between the constitutional guarantee in the US and the reality of public defenders carrying impossible caseloads is a rather damning illustration. We don't even have the constitutional guarantee, which makes the political will to fund it even more fragile. Right, I think I have enough to begin preparing my remarks. I may come back to you for help sharpening the statistics before the panel.

Please do. I'd be happy to help you refine the figures and structure the argument when you're closer to the date. You clearly have both the expertise and the conviction to make a compelling case — it's just a matter of organising the data so it lands with the audience. Good luck with the preparation.
